days.content:
You can trigger mostly small avalanches on previously wind-drifted slopes. The most concerning slopes face north, northeast, and east at higher elevations.

If you experience any cracking or collapsing underneath you, then steer clear of slopes steeper than about 30 degrees. You can find safer terrain at lower elevations, in wind-sheltered locations, and on southerly and westerly-facing slopes where any recent snow fell onto bare ground.

Images.caption: This snowpit was dug on Wolf Creek Pass on December 2 on a north-facing slope near treeline. Here, the snowpack is 70 centimeters deep with a distinct layer of buried surface hoar about 25 centimeters down in the snowpack. Currently, there is not enough of a slab on top for this to be concerning, but as more snow falls this week in this area, we could see Persistent Slab avalanches failing on this layer. Wolf Creek Pass, 12/2/2025.
Images.altText: A snowpit with a distinct layer of buried surface hoar in it.

Images.caption: While this avalanche occurred near Red Mountain Pass in the northern San Juan Mountains, it's a good illustration of the type of Persistent Slab avalanche you could trigger if you find a deeply drifted slab over old weak snow above treeline. Skiers travelling on the flat bench above this slope remotely triggered it. As you can see, following this last storm, the size of potential avalanches is growing and the character of our Persistent Slab problem is changing. Red Mountain Pass, 12/1/2025.
Images.altText: A slab avalanche with some ski tracks nearby.

Images.caption: This avalanche happened before the last storm, where wind-stiffened snow rested over weak snow. The fresh blanket we received from the last storm has made these places harder to identify, but give a wide berth to freshly-loaded deeper drifts, especially around rocky outcrops. Red Mountain Pass, 11/29/2025.
